name: NTSB Marine Weather Root Cause ACH Probe

hypotheses.label: Sudden severe weather generated towline force that overwhelmed maneuverability and stability
A preexisting equipment or structural failure initiated the casualty
A barge cargo, draft, or stability problem initiated the casualty
The primary cause was avoidable failure to receive or act on weather warnings

evidence.label: NTSB probable cause attributes the casualty to overwhelming towline force from the towed barge during sudden severe weather
Nearest NWS station reported thunderstorms at KGLS, heavy rain, 48-knot gusts, and 0.85 inches rainfall

source.source_row: weather observation source row recovered by QA q015

evidence.label: The vessel rapidly lost speed, heeled to port, and the crew described the towline pulling the tug sideways/backwards during the storm
Post-casualty survey found no cargo damage and draft mark readings were per the approved transit stability condition
Investigators found superstructure fractures and indentations that appeared to be from recovery activities
The mate did not recall hearing updated warnings, but the report says the crew likely could not have avoided the storm even if they had received them

judgments.rationale: This is the report's direct causal conclusion.
The conclusion names weather-driven towline force, not a preexisting equipment or structural initiating failure.
The conclusion attributes the casualty to force generated by the towed barge during weather, not to defective cargo or draft/stability setup.
The probable-cause sentence does not by itself decide whether warning awareness was avoidable or causal.
Severe weather observations are compatible with weather force overwhelming the tow.
Bad weather does not alone rule out a simultaneous equipment or structural problem.
The weather report does not directly test cargo, draft, or barge stability.
Severe weather warnings and observations are compatible with a warning-awareness hypothesis.
The speed drop, heeling, and tight towline description fit the weather/towline-force mechanism.
Loss of speed and heeling could coexist with equipment problems, but the evidence points more directly to external force.
The observation names force from the tow and barge position but not a cargo/draft defect.
The physical sequence does not determine whether earlier warning awareness would have changed the outcome.
Normal cargo/draft findings are compatible with the barge acting as a wind-loaded tow rather than a defective load.
Barge condition does not directly decide tug equipment or structure.
No cargo damage and approved draft readings disconfirm a barge cargo/draft/stability initiating problem.
Barge condition does not directly decide warning awareness.
Damage attributed to recovery activity leaves the weather/towline mechanism intact.
Damage seen after salvage is not evidence of a preexisting structural initiating failure.
Recovery-related tug damage does not directly decide barge cargo or draft condition.
Recovery-related damage does not bear on warning awareness.
The report says the crew likely could not avoid the storm, reinforcing the physical-force explanation.
Weather-warning limits do not directly decide equipment or structure.
Weather-warning limits do not directly decide cargo or draft condition.
The report explicitly limits a warning-awareness causal theory by saying avoidance likely was not possible even with the warnings.
